behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the heuristic algorithm itself (route quality ratio), not OS/network/subprocess or model output. A deterministic in-process test can generate fixed coordinate sets, compute heuristic route length, compare against an exact/benchmark optimal solver result fixture, and catch real regressions in heuristic quality. Higher levels were considered and rejected: behavior text is explicitly about heuristic output quality, with no geocoding/API/runtime infrastructure dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Call the TSP heuristic function directly with deterministic stop-coordinate fixtures (including edge layouts like clustered, line, and mixed). For each case, compute/lookup optimal length and assert heuristic_length/optimal_length <= 1.5 for n<=20.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is most likely to fail at module handoff: distance output + road-type speed config + estimator math/unit conversion. No real OS/network/subprocess is required, and no AI/ML output is involved. System/Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text (pure deterministic calculation from distance and config).
behaviors.test_description: Wire real local modules for (a) distance provider output objects, (b) road-type speed configuration lookup, and (c) time-window estimator. Feed a multi-leg route with mixed road types and assert per-leg and cumulative time windows match expected values (including unit conversion and rounding rules).

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is a deterministic validation rule (sum/compare/compute excess) at a single object/function boundary. No cross-module infrastructure is required to expose the core failure. Higher levels rejected because behavior text does not require real I/O, external process, or model-dependent judgment.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the validator directly with crafted estimated-duration totals and remaining-shift values. Assert rejection when total > remaining and exact excess value is returned; assert acceptance at boundary equality and below-boundary cases.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in orchestration across modules: geocoding-result handling, filtered stop list, optimizer invocation, and skipped-address reporting. A deterministic stub geocoding adapter returning "no results" reproduces the failure mode. System was considered but rejected because behavior text specifies logical handling of a known API result shape, not real network/timeout/HTTP transport behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service layer end-to-end in-process with a fake geocoding client that returns coordinates for most addresses and empty result for one. Assert output route excludes failed address, optimizer runs on remaining stops, and response includes the skipped-address list exactly.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Real failure often appears at handoff between deduplication/aggregation and route planning (duplicates may be merged in one module but still emitted twice downstream). No real OS/network/subprocess or AI output dependency is needed. System/Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by deterministic local data-processing behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Execute input-normalization + item-aggregation + route-construction modules together on input containing duplicate addresses with distinct item lists. Assert one stop exists for that address, items are combined correctly, and final route contains exactly one visit for that location.
